question: Which Georgia statute governs the licensing and regulation of real estate licensees?
choices: O.C.G.A. Title 48, Chapter 6
O.C.G.A. Title 44, Chapter 14
O.C.G.A. Title 10, Chapter 1
O.C.G.A. Title 43, Chapter 40
explanation: O.C.G.A. Title 43, Chapter 40 is the Georgia Real Estate License Law — the primary statute that establishes licensing requirements, conduct standards, and the authority of the Georgia Real Estate Commission.

question: The Georgia Real Estate Commission (GREC) is attached to which state agency for administrative purposes?
choices: Department of Community Affairs
Department of Revenue
Department of Banking and Finance
Office of the Secretary of State
explanation: GREC is attached to the Office of the Secretary of State for administrative purposes only. The Commission itself retains independent authority over licensing and discipline.

question: How many members serve on the Georgia Real Estate Commission?
choices: Six members
Seven members
Five members
Nine members
explanation: The Georgia Real Estate Commission is composed of seven members: six licensed real estate brokers (one from each congressional district) and one consumer member, all appointed by the Governor.

question: A Georgia salesperson license applicant must complete how many hours of pre-license education?
choices: 90 hours
60 hours
75 hours
40 hours
explanation: Georgia requires 75 hours of pre-license education for salesperson applicants before they are eligible to sit for the state licensing exam. The coursework must be completed at a GREC-approved school.

question: Under Georgia law, an active salesperson license must be held under the supervision of a:
choices: Branch office manager
Real estate attorney
Licensed associate broker
Qualifying broker
explanation: In Georgia, every salesperson must be affiliated with and supervised by a qualifying broker. The qualifying broker is responsible for the activities of all licensees in the firm.

question: Which of the following activities requires a Georgia real estate license?
choices: Listing and selling property for others for a fee
Selling your own personally owned property
Auctioning personal property at an estate sale
Managing properties for a family member at no compensation
explanation: A license is required to perform real estate brokerage activities — including listing and selling real property for others — for compensation. Selling your own property, managing a family member's property without pay, and auctioning personal (non-real) property are exempted activities.

question: The Georgia Real Estate Education, Research and Recovery Fund (RERF) is primarily funded by:
choices: License fees paid by Georgia licensees
Legislative appropriations from the state general fund
Federal HUD grants
A percentage of real estate transfer taxes
explanation: The Georgia Real Estate Education, Research, and Recovery Fund is funded primarily by a portion of the fees paid by licensees at initial application and renewal. The fund compensates consumers harmed by licensee misconduct when civil judgments are uncollectible.

question: What is the maximum amount a single claimant may recover from the Georgia Real Estate Education, Research and Recovery Fund?
explanation: The maximum any single claimant may recover from the Georgia Real Estate Recovery Fund is $35,000. There is also a $75,000 aggregate limit against any one licensee.

question: After the Real Estate Recovery Fund pays a claim on behalf of a licensee, the most likely consequence for that licensee is:
choices: A written reprimand and mandatory continuing education
Automatic revocation of the license until the fund is fully repaid
Probation with enhanced supervision for two years
Suspension for 90 days and a fine equal to the amount paid
explanation: When the Recovery Fund pays a claim on behalf of a licensee, Georgia law requires that the licensee's license be automatically revoked. The license may not be reinstated until the licensee has repaid the fund in full, with interest.

question: Georgia sales agent licenses must be renewed every:
choices: Four years
One year
Three years
Two years
explanation: Georgia real estate licenses — both salesperson and broker — operate on a four-year renewal cycle. Licensees must complete 36 hours of continuing education, including a mandatory 3-hour License Law course, before each renewal.

question: Which of the following disclosures is required by Georgia law in every real estate advertisement by a licensee?
choices: The firm name as it appears on the license
The number of years the licensee has been licensed
The licensee's personal cell phone number
The licensee's personal home address
explanation: Georgia's advertising rules require that all real estate advertising by a licensee include the name of the real estate firm as it appears on the license. Personal addresses, years of experience, and personal cell numbers are not required disclosures.

question: Under Georgia license law, 'commingling' refers to a licensee:
choices: Representing both buyer and seller in the same transaction
Splitting commission with an unlicensed person
Mixing client funds with the licensee's personal or firm operating funds
Marketing property outside the licensee's geographic territory
explanation: Commingling is the illegal act of mixing client or customer funds (such as earnest money) with the licensee's or firm's own operating or personal funds. Georgia requires that trust funds be maintained in a separate escrow account.

question: A Georgia real estate licensee who engages in 'blockbusting' is subject to discipline because it violates:
choices: The Georgia Uniform Land Sales Practices Act
The Georgia Environmental Policy Act
Georgia's Fair Housing law and the federal Fair Housing Act
The Georgia Property Owners' Association Act
explanation: Blockbusting — inducing owners to sell by making representations about the entry of protected-class persons into a neighborhood — violates both the federal Fair Housing Act and Georgia's state fair housing laws, and constitutes grounds for license discipline.

question: Which of the following persons is EXEMPT from Georgia real estate licensing requirements?
choices: A property manager who negotiates leases on behalf of the owner
A licensed auctioneer who sells real property at auction for a fee
A resident apartment manager employed by and managing a single complex
A real estate investor who markets a subdivision to the public
explanation: Georgia exempts salaried resident apartment managers employed directly by the property owner to manage a single complex. Property managers who negotiate leases for others, subdivision marketers, and auctioneers selling real estate for compensation generally need a real estate license.

question: A Georgia broker who fails to renew the license before expiration and continues to conduct brokerage activities is:
choices: Only liable for a late fee with no other consequence
Practicing real estate without a license, which is a criminal offense
Operating under a grace period and is technically compliant
Allowed to operate if an application for renewal is pending
explanation: Practicing real estate in Georgia without a current, active license is a criminal offense. There is no statutory grace period for conducting brokerage activities after a license expires; continuing to act would constitute unlicensed practice.

question: Under Georgia law, a qualifying broker who terminates a salesperson must notify the Georgia Real Estate Commission within:
choices: 3 business days
10 days
24 hours
30 days
explanation: When a salesperson's affiliation with a qualifying broker is terminated, the broker must notify GREC within 10 days. The salesperson's license is returned to an inactive status until they affiliate with a new qualifying broker.

question: The Georgia Brokerage Relationships in Real Estate Transactions Act (BRRETA) governs:
choices: Subdivision development and plat recording requirements
The calculation and payment of real estate commissions
The legal relationships between brokers and their clients and customers
Environmental disclosure requirements for all property sales
explanation: BRRETA establishes the legal framework for brokerage relationships in Georgia — defining client and customer relationships, the duties owed under each, and how agency is created and disclosed. It does not govern commissions, environmental disclosures, or subdivisions.

question: Under BRRETA, a 'client' is defined as a person who has entered into a:
choices: Brokerage engagement agreement with a broker
Listing agreement directly with the MLS
Purchase contract with a seller
Mortgage commitment with a lender
explanation: Under BRRETA, a client is a person who has signed a brokerage engagement agreement — either a listing agreement (seller client) or a buyer brokerage agreement (buyer client). Persons without such an agreement are 'customers' who receive limited, non-fiduciary services.

question: BRRETA requires that a broker provide a client with which of the following duties?
choices: All fiduciary duties including undivided loyalty and unlimited confidentiality
Accounting, confidentiality, obedience, loyalty, and disclosure
Timely presentation of offers, promotion of the client's best interests, and accounting for money
Honesty, presenting all offers, and informing the client about defects known to the broker
explanation: BRRETA specifies that a broker owes a client: timely presentation of offers, promotion of the client's best interests, disclosure of material adverse facts, accounting for money and property, and compliance with the brokerage engagement. Georgia uses a statutory duties framework rather than the common-law fiduciary label.

question: Under BRRETA, a Georgia broker acting as a 'transaction broker' (facilitator) owes the parties:
choices: Only the duties owed to customers — honesty, presenting offers, and disclosing material defects
Confidentiality to the seller only, because the seller pays the commission
Full fiduciary duties to both the buyer and the seller equally
No duties whatsoever, since neither party is a client
explanation: When a broker acts as a transaction broker or facilitator, neither party is a client; both are customers. BRRETA limits the broker's duties to: honesty, presenting all written offers, disclosing adverse material facts known to the broker, and complying with the law. Full fiduciary duties are not owed.

question: Georgia's Seller's Property Disclosure Statement is:
choices: Not required by statute but commonly used to protect sellers from post-closing claims
Required only when the seller has actual knowledge of material defects
Required only if the property was built before 1978
Mandatory for all residential sales of one-to-four family dwellings
explanation: Georgia does not mandate a seller disclosure statement by statute. However, the GREC-approved Seller's Property Disclosure Statement is widely used as a best practice to document the seller's knowledge of property conditions and protect sellers from post-closing liability.

question: Under BRRETA, when must a Georgia broker provide a written brokerage engagement to a prospective client?
choices: Only if the transaction involves property over $100,000
At or before the time the client signs an offer or listing agreement
Within three business days of the first showing
Before the first substantive contact with the client
explanation: BRRETA requires that the written brokerage engagement be signed at or before the time the client signs an offer to purchase or a listing agreement. Georgia does not require written disclosure before the first substantive contact as some other states do.

question: A Georgia real estate licensee discovers a material defect in a listed property that the seller is unaware of. Under BRRETA, the licensee must:
choices: Disclose the defect to prospective buyers even without the seller's consent
Disclose the defect only if asked directly by a buyer
Keep the defect confidential because it was discovered while representing the seller
Withhold disclosure until after an offer is received
explanation: Georgia BRRETA requires licensees to disclose adverse material facts that are known to the broker — even if representing the seller — because these facts must be disclosed to all parties. Client confidentiality does not extend to material defects that could harm a buyer.

question: In Georgia, 'designated agency' under BRRETA means:
choices: One licensee is designated to handle all the firm's transactions for a given year
A licensee is designated by GREC to handle distressed properties
Different affiliated licensees within the same firm each represent one party exclusively
The broker personally represents both buyer and seller in the same transaction
explanation: Georgia's designated agency allows a firm to appoint separate affiliated licensees to represent the buyer and the seller exclusively within the same firm and transaction. The broker then acts as a dual agent at the brokerage level, but each client still receives exclusive representation from their designated agent.

question: Which of the following must a Georgia listing agent disclose to a prospective buyer under BRRETA?
choices: The seller's minimum acceptable price
Confidential terms of the listing agreement
A known structural defect in the property
The seller's motivation for selling
explanation: BRRETA requires all licensees (regardless of representation) to disclose adverse material facts known to them, including known structural defects. The seller's minimum price, motivation, and confidential listing terms are client confidences that must not be disclosed.

question: Under Georgia law, an exclusive right-to-sell listing agreement must include:
choices: MLS enrollment within 24 hours of signing
A definite expiration date
A guaranteed minimum commission amount
A provision allowing the seller to cancel at will
explanation: Georgia requires that every exclusive listing agreement contain a definite expiration date. Open-ended or 'until notice' listings are prohibited because they could bind the seller indefinitely without a clear termination right.

question: The Georgia Association of REALTORS® (GAR) Purchase and Sale Agreement is best described as:
choices: A government-approved form that supersedes other written agreements
A preliminary agreement that must be followed by a GREC-approved final contract
An industry-standard contract form published by the professional association
A state-mandated form required for all residential sales
explanation: The GAR Purchase and Sale Agreement is an industry-standard contract form created by the Georgia Association of REALTORS®. It is widely used in Georgia residential transactions but is not mandated by state law; parties may use any mutually agreed-upon written contract.

question: In Georgia, real estate contracts for the sale of land must be:
choices: Filed with the county recorder within 30 days
Approved by a real estate attorney before closing
Notarized to be enforceable
In writing and signed by the parties to be enforceable
explanation: Georgia's Statute of Frauds (O.C.G.A. § 13-5-30) requires that contracts for the sale of real property be in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. Notarization, recording, and attorney approval are not required for the contract to be enforceable.

question: Georgia property management requires a real estate license when the manager:
choices: Collects dues for a homeowners association of which they are a member
Maintains a residential property owned solely by a family member
Acts as a salaried employee managing a single complex owned by the employer
Manages property for others and receives compensation for those services
explanation: A real estate license is required in Georgia for anyone who manages property for others for compensation, including negotiating leases and collecting rent. The exemptions cover salaried employees managing a single employer-owned complex, personal family property, and HOA dues collection by owners.

question: A Georgia broker received an earnest money deposit. Under state law and GREC rules, the broker must place the funds in an escrow account within:
choices: 5 business days of receipt
3 banking days of receipt
The same day as contract ratification
24 hours of receipt
explanation: Georgia rules require that a broker deposit earnest money into a trust or escrow account within three banking days of receipt. Failure to timely deposit trust funds is grounds for disciplinary action.

question: Under Georgia law, a real estate commission is earned when the broker has:
choices: Been paid by the listing firm regardless of closing
Produced a ready, willing, and able buyer who meets all the seller's terms
Obtained a ratified contract regardless of whether closing occurs
Successfully closed the transaction and title has transferred
explanation: Under the traditional Georgia common-law rule, a broker earns a commission when they produce a ready, willing, and able buyer on the seller's terms, even if closing does not occur due to the seller's default. However, many modern listing agreements now condition payment on closing.

question: Which of the following fair housing violations would be unique to a Georgia state-law claim (not solely federal)?
choices: Discriminating based on familial status in a non-exempt property
Refusing to rent to a Section 8 voucher holder where Georgia law prohibits source-of-income discrimination
Steering buyers away from neighborhoods based on national origin
Refusing to rent based on race
explanation: Federal fair housing law does not protect source-of-income as a class. Georgia law in some jurisdictions prohibits discrimination based on source of income (such as housing vouchers). Discrimination based on race, national origin, and familial status are covered by both federal and Georgia law.

question: Which document in a Georgia residential transaction typically discloses whether the property is in a homeowners association?
choices: The Georgia Residential Mortgage Act disclosure
The lender's commitment letter
The Seller's Property Disclosure Statement or the GAR Purchase and Sale Agreement
The plat recorded with the county
explanation: The GAR Purchase and Sale Agreement and Seller's Property Disclosure Statement include sections addressing HOA membership, dues, and assessments. Licensees have a duty to disclose known HOA information to buyers.

question: Under Georgia law, a broker who has a written brokerage engagement with a buyer shows the buyer a property listed by the broker's own firm. This situation creates:
choices: Automatic termination of the buyer brokerage engagement
A disclosed dual-agency or designated-agency situation requiring written consent
An illegal conflict of interest requiring the buyer to hire a different broker
No conflict because the listing and buyer agreements are separate documents
explanation: When a broker's firm represents both the buyer client and the seller client in the same transaction, Georgia BRRETA requires disclosure and written informed consent for dual agency (or, alternatively, the use of designated agency where different licensees in the firm represent each party).

question: In Georgia, a licensee acting as a buyer's agent who learns confidential information about the buyer's motivation and maximum price must:
choices: Disclose it to the seller immediately as a material fact
Disclose it to the listing agent to facilitate negotiations
Share it with the listing agent if asked directly
Keep it confidential even after the transaction closes
explanation: BRRETA's duty of confidentiality survives termination of the brokerage engagement. A buyer's agent must not disclose the buyer's financial position, motivation, or maximum price to the seller or listing agent — doing so would breach the duty owed to the client.

question: Georgia's 'as-is' clause in a purchase contract means:
choices: The seller has no obligation to disclose known defects
The buyer accepts the property in its current condition but does not waive the right to inspect or sue for fraud
The buyer cannot negotiate repairs or price reductions after the inspection
The seller is released from all liability after closing
explanation: An 'as-is' clause in Georgia means the buyer accepts the property in its present condition; however, it does not relieve the seller or listing agent of the obligation to disclose known material defects, nor does it bar claims of fraud or misrepresentation. Buyers retain the right to inspect.

question: Under the Georgia Residential Mortgage Act, a person who negotiates mortgage loans for consumers in Georgia must be licensed as a:
choices: Licensed attorney with a real estate practice certification
Real estate broker with a mortgage endorsement
Mortgage loan originator under the Nationwide Multistate Licensing System (NMLS)
Registered investment adviser
explanation: The Georgia Residential Mortgage Act requires that individuals who originate or negotiate residential mortgage loans for consumers register as mortgage loan originators through the NMLS. Real estate licensees may not originate loans without a separate mortgage license.

question: Georgia's Abandoned Mobile Home Act primarily addresses:
choices: Zoning restrictions on mobile home parks
Federal HUD construction standards for manufactured housing
Lending requirements for mobile home purchases
The process for removing titles and disposing of abandoned manufactured homes
explanation: Georgia's Abandoned Mobile Home Act establishes the legal process by which counties and municipalities may remove titles from and dispose of abandoned manufactured homes. It is a state-specific law relevant to real estate practice involving manufactured housing.

question: Under Georgia law, a real estate licensee who advertises a property on social media must include:
choices: The licensee's license number
A statement that the content was created by a licensed agent
The listing price and MLS number
The name of the brokerage firm as it appears on the license
explanation: GREC advertising rules require that all advertising — including social media — include the name of the real estate firm as it appears on the license. Omitting the firm name in any advertisement is a violation.

question: A Georgia salesperson who wants to become a broker must have at least how many years of active licensure experience?
choices: Five years
One year
Two years
Three years
explanation: To upgrade from salesperson to broker in Georgia, the applicant must have at least three years of active real estate licensure experience within the preceding five years, in addition to completing the required 60-hour broker pre-license course.

question: Georgia is classified as which type of foreclosure state?
choices: Hybrid — either judicial or non-judicial at the lender's election
Non-judicial (power-of-sale) foreclosure
Judicial foreclosure only
Strict foreclosure
explanation: Georgia is a non-judicial (power-of-sale) foreclosure state. Lenders may foreclose by advertising and sale without going to court, as long as the required notice and publication procedures under O.C.G.A. § 44-14-162 are followed. This makes the process relatively fast compared to judicial states.

question: Under Georgia's non-judicial foreclosure process, a lender must publish notice of sale for how many consecutive weeks in the official county newspaper?
choices: Two weeks
Six weeks
One week
Four weeks
explanation: Georgia law (O.C.G.A. § 44-14-162.2) requires that notice of a non-judicial foreclosure sale be advertised for four consecutive weeks in the official newspaper of the county where the property is located, with the sale held on the first Tuesday of a month.

question: In Georgia, real estate closings are typically conducted by:
choices: The listing real estate broker
A HUD-certified housing counselor
A licensed attorney
A title insurance company acting as its own settlement agent
explanation: Georgia is an attorney-closing state. The Georgia Supreme Court's unauthorized-practice-of-law rules require that real estate closings be conducted by a licensed Georgia attorney. The attorney examines title, prepares closing documents, and disburses funds.

question: Georgia's transfer tax (real estate excise tax) is calculated at:
choices: 1% of the mortgage amount
$1.00 per $500 of the sale price or fraction thereof
0.5% of the sale price
$1.00 per $1,000 of the sale price or fraction thereof
explanation: Georgia imposes a real estate transfer tax of $1.00 per $500 (or fraction of $500) of the consideration. For example, a $200,000 sale would generate a transfer tax of $400. The tax is imposed on the grantor (seller).

question: Who typically pays the Georgia real estate transfer tax?
choices: It is split 50/50 between buyer and seller
The seller
The buyer
The real estate broker
explanation: By statute, the Georgia real estate transfer tax is imposed on the grantor — the seller. As with most closing costs, it may be negotiated between the parties, but the statutory default is that the seller pays.

question: Georgia's FIRPTA withholding obligation applies when:
choices: The buyer is a foreign national purchasing property valued over $1 million
The property is located in a special flood hazard area
The mortgage lender is a foreign institution
The seller is a foreign national or non-resident alien selling U.S. real property
explanation: FIRPTA (Federal law, also enforced at closing by Georgia attorneys) requires withholding of 15% of the gross sales price when the seller is a foreign person (non-resident alien). The buyer/closing attorney is the withholding agent and must remit to the IRS unless an exemption applies.

question: In a Georgia closing, property taxes are typically prorated:
choices: Based on the prior year's assessed value using a 360-day year
Based on the current year's tax bill or estimated taxes using a 365-day year
Only if required by the buyer's lender
After closing, with the county tax commissioner adjusting the bill
explanation: Georgia property taxes are paid in arrears. At closing, taxes are typically prorated using the current year's tax bill (or last year's bill as an estimate if the current bill is not yet issued) on a 365-day basis through the date of closing, with the seller crediting the buyer for the seller's portion.

question: A Georgia intangible recording tax is imposed on:
choices: Long-term real estate notes and mortgage instruments at the time of recording
Deed transfers between related parties
Capital gains realized from real property sales
The sale price of all real property transfers
explanation: Georgia imposes an intangible recording tax on long-term notes secured by real estate at the rate of $1.50 per $500 of the loan principal (rounded up). This is paid at the time the security deed is recorded and is typically a buyer/borrower closing cost.

question: In Georgia, the instrument used to pledge real property as security for a mortgage loan is called a:
choices: Security deed (deed to secure debt)
Mortgage deed
Warranty deed with power of sale
Deed of trust
explanation: Georgia uses the security deed (also called a 'deed to secure debt' or 'deed of trust' colloquially, but technically a security deed) rather than a traditional mortgage. Title is conveyed to the lender as security and reconveyed upon payoff, which facilitates Georgia's non-judicial foreclosure process.

question: Georgia's usury laws cap certain residential mortgage interest rates, but conventional lenders are most often exempt because:
choices: Georgia has no usury laws for any type of loan
Federal preemption under the Depository Institutions Deregulation Act exempts federally chartered lenders from state usury limits on first mortgage loans
Borrowers must individually waive usury protections in writing
The Federal Reserve sets a maximum rate that preempts state caps
explanation: Federal preemption under the Depository Institutions Deregulation and Monetary Control Act (DIDMCA) allows federally chartered banks, savings associations, and credit unions to charge market rates on first-lien residential mortgages, effectively exempting them from Georgia's state usury ceilings.

question: Under Georgia license law, a licensee who pays an unlicensed person a referral fee for sending clients is guilty of:
choices: A violation only if the referral fee exceeds $500
An ethical violation reviewable only by a professional association
Unlawful sharing of compensation with an unlicensed person
A civil infraction subject only to a monetary fine
explanation: Georgia law prohibits sharing real estate compensation with any person who is not licensed under Chapter 40. Paying a referral fee to an unlicensed person constitutes an unlawful sharing of compensation and is grounds for disciplinary action regardless of the amount.

question: The Georgia Real Estate Commission has authority to take which of the following disciplinary actions against a licensee?
choices: Order restitution and revoke a licensee's professional liability insurance
Revoke, suspend, or place on probation a licensee's license
Impose criminal fines and imprisonment
Impose jail sentences and mandatory community service
explanation: GREC's disciplinary authority includes revoking, suspending, or placing on probation a license, and may also include fines and required education. GREC is an administrative agency and cannot impose criminal penalties such as imprisonment.

question: Which of the following is a TRUE statement about continuing education (CE) requirements for Georgia licensees?
choices: 24 hours of CE are required annually, with no mandatory specific topics
CE is optional; only the pre-license education is mandatory
CE is only required when upgrading from salesperson to broker
36 hours of CE are required every four years, including a mandatory 3-hour License Law course
explanation: Georgia requires 36 hours of continuing education during each four-year renewal period. Included in those 36 hours must be a mandatory 3-hour License Law and Rules course. Failure to complete CE prevents renewal.

question: A Georgia real estate license is required to sell which type of property?
choices: Mineral rights attached to property owned by the seller
A multi-unit apartment building for another owner for a commission
A single condominium unit owned personally by the seller
A cemetery lot owned personally by the seller
explanation: Selling, leasing, or managing real property for others for compensation requires a Georgia real estate license. Selling your own property (cemetery lots, condominiums, or mineral rights you personally own) is exempt from the licensing requirement.

question: When a Georgia licensee changes their mailing address, they must notify GREC within:
choices: 24 hours
60 days
30 days
10 days
explanation: GREC rules require that licensees notify the Commission of a change of address within 30 days. Because GREC communicates with licensees at their address of record, failure to update the address can result in missed notices and disciplinary consequences.

question: In Georgia, 'conversion' of client funds by a licensee refers to:
choices: Changing a listing from exclusive right-to-sell to open listing
Converting a fixed-rate mortgage to an adjustable rate
Converting a residential property to a commercial use
Using client or escrow funds for the licensee's personal or business purposes
explanation: Conversion is the misappropriation of funds held in trust — using client money (such as earnest money or security deposits) for the licensee's own personal or business purposes. It is a serious violation that typically results in license revocation and may involve criminal charges.

question: Which of the following persons does NOT need a Georgia real estate license?
choices: A buyer's representative negotiating purchases for corporate clients
An auctioneer who sells real property at auction for compensation
A property manager who negotiates residential leases for multiple owners
A licensed Georgia attorney acting in the course of their legal practice
explanation: Georgia exempts licensed attorneys who perform real estate activities in the course of practicing law. Property managers negotiating leases for others, auctioneers selling real estate for compensation, and buyer's representatives for others all require a real estate license.

question: A Georgia qualifying broker who operates from multiple office locations must:
choices: Designate a separate qualifying broker for each branch office
Obtain GREC's advance written approval for each new office
Register each branch office with GREC and display the firm's license at each location
Hold separate licenses for each office location
explanation: A firm may operate multiple branch offices, but each location must be registered with GREC and the firm's license (or a branch certificate) must be displayed at each location. The qualifying broker may supervise all branches; a separate qualifying broker per branch is not required.

question: Under Georgia rules, which of the following is required before a real estate school may advertise a pre-license course?
choices: GREC approval of the school and its instructors
A surety bond equal to one year's tuition revenue
Accreditation by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools
A minimum enrollment guarantee of 20 students per course
explanation: Real estate schools offering pre-license and continuing education courses in Georgia must be approved by GREC. Both the school and its instructors must meet GREC's qualifications; unapproved coursework does not count toward licensing requirements.

question: Georgia's license law prohibits a licensee from engaging in 'steering,' which is defined as:
choices: Recommending specific lenders or title companies to buyers
Directing buyers toward or away from neighborhoods based on protected class characteristics
Showing buyers properties outside their stated price range
Advising sellers to set an asking price above fair market value
explanation: Steering is the illegal practice of guiding buyers toward or away from particular neighborhoods based on the race, color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, disability, or other protected characteristics of the residents. It violates the Georgia Fair Housing Act and the federal Fair Housing Act.

question: How long must a Georgia broker retain transaction records under GREC rules?
choices: Seven years from the date of closing
Five years from the date of closing or termination of the listing
Three years from the date of the transaction
One year from the date of closing
explanation: GREC rules require brokers to retain real estate transaction records — including contracts, closing statements, and correspondence — for three years. These records must be available for inspection by GREC upon request.

question: A Georgia licensee acting as a property manager collects the first month's rent, the last month's rent, and a security deposit. Which of these amounts must be placed in a separate escrow account?
choices: Only the first and last month's rent
All three amounts must be commingled in a single operating account
The security deposit and any advance rents held on behalf of the owner
Only the security deposit
explanation: Under Georgia law, security deposits and funds held on behalf of owners (including advance rent paid before the period it covers) must be held in a trust or escrow account separate from the licensee's operating funds. The distinction between earned rent and held funds is critical.

question: A Georgia real estate license applicant who has been convicted of a felony involving fraud:
choices: May still apply, and GREC will consider the nature and recency of the conviction
Is permanently and automatically barred from licensure
Must wait exactly 10 years after conviction before applying
Is eligible only for an inactive license
explanation: A felony conviction does not automatically bar Georgia licensure. GREC reviews each application individually, weighing the nature of the crime, time elapsed, rehabilitation evidence, and relevance to real estate practice. GREC may deny, grant, or grant with conditions.

question: Under Georgia law, which of the following constitutes grounds for disciplinary action against a licensee?
choices: Advertising property without including the broker's firm name
Accepting a listing below the neighborhood's average market value
Representing both a buyer and seller in the same transaction with full written consent
Showing a property more than twice without an accepted offer
explanation: Advertising real property without including the firm's name as it appears on the license violates GREC advertising rules and is grounds for disciplinary action. Dual representation with consent, below-market listings, and multiple showings are all lawful practices.

question: A Georgia salesperson who wants to operate an independent brokerage must first:
choices: Operate under a qualifying broker for at least five years
Obtain a broker's license
Complete an additional 40-hour management course
Register a sole-proprietorship trade name with GREC
explanation: Only a licensed broker may operate an independent real estate brokerage in Georgia. A salesperson must upgrade to a broker's license (requiring three years of active licensure and completing the 60-hour broker course) before acting as an independent broker or firm owner.

question: Which of the following is required for a valid Georgia real estate license application?
choices: Proof of a four-year college degree
A minimum of two professional letters of recommendation
Successful completion of the 75-hour pre-license course and passing the state licensing exam
Proof of Errors and Omissions insurance coverage
explanation: Georgia requires completion of the 75-hour pre-license course from a GREC-approved school and passing the PSI/AMP state licensing exam. A college degree, recommendation letters, and E&O insurance are not statutory requirements for initial licensure.

question: Under BRRETA, a Georgia broker who represents a seller must disclose to a prospective buyer that the property is in a flood zone:
choices: Only if the buyer specifically asks about flood risk
Only in the closing disclosure, not during marketing
Because flood zone status is an adverse material fact known to the broker
Only if the property has experienced flooding in the past 10 years
explanation: BRRETA requires disclosure of adverse material facts known to the broker regardless of which party the broker represents. Flood zone designation is a material fact affecting value and insurability that must be disclosed to prospective buyers.

question: In Georgia, the term 'customer' under BRRETA refers to:
choices: A person who has signed a buyer brokerage agreement
A person who interacts with a broker but has not signed a brokerage engagement
Any person who pays the commission in a transaction
A person who has submitted a written offer to purchase property
explanation: Under BRRETA, a 'customer' is any person who receives real estate services from a broker without having entered into a written brokerage engagement. Customers receive honesty, lawful services, and disclosure of material defects, but not the full range of duties owed to clients.

question: A buyer's agent in Georgia negotiates a purchase on behalf of the buyer. After the transaction closes, the buyer's agent receives a bonus from the seller's agent for 'helping the deal close.' This is:
choices: Standard practice regulated by the MLS rules
A breach of the duty of loyalty to the buyer client
Permissible because the transaction has already closed
Permissible only if the buyer agent discloses it to the buyer in advance
explanation: Accepting undisclosed compensation from the opposing party is a breach of the duty owed to the buyer client under BRRETA. A buyer's agent must act in the buyer's best interests; accepting hidden payments from or arranged by the seller creates an undisclosed conflict of interest.

question: Under Georgia law, when a tenant-occupied property is listed for sale, the listing agent should:
choices: Show the property only after the tenant vacates the premises
Require the seller to evict all tenants before the listing goes active
Disclose the existence of tenants and obtain proper consent before entry
Disclose the tenants' identities and lease terms to all prospective buyers
explanation: Tenant-occupied listings require the broker to disclose that the property is tenant-occupied, comply with Georgia landlord-tenant law regarding proper notice before entry (typically 24 hours), and respect tenant privacy. Requiring eviction or disclosing personal tenant information would be improper.

question: Which type of listing agreement gives the listing broker the exclusive right to earn a commission regardless of who sells the property?
choices: Exclusive right-to-sell listing
Net listing
Exclusive agency listing
Open listing
explanation: An exclusive right-to-sell listing guarantees the broker a commission if the property sells during the listing period, regardless of who procures the buyer — including the seller themselves. Georgia NAR members are prohibited from taking net listings, as they create an inherent conflict of interest.

question: The Georgia Association of REALTORS® (GAR) contract forms include a 'due diligence' or 'inspection contingency' period during which the buyer may:
choices: Extend the closing date by up to 30 days as of right
Request repairs but cannot cancel without forfeiting earnest money
Cancel the contract for any reason and receive a refund of earnest money
Only cancel if the inspection reveals a structural defect costing more than 5% of purchase price
explanation: Under the standard GAR Due Diligence / Inspection Contingency, the buyer may terminate the contract for any reason during the due diligence period and receive a full refund of earnest money. The seller must respond to repair requests but the buyer retains the absolute right to cancel.

question: Georgia's Landlord-Tenant Act (O.C.G.A. § 44-7) requires a residential landlord to:
choices: Provide a written lease for all tenancies over 30 days
Return a security deposit within 30 days after the tenant vacates, with an itemized statement
Allow tenants to withhold rent for any habitability defect
Provide a minimum of 90 days notice before a rent increase
explanation: Georgia law requires landlords to return security deposits within one month (30 days) after the tenant surrenders the property, along with an itemized written statement of any deductions. Failure to comply may result in the landlord forfeiting the right to retain any portion of the deposit.

question: Under Georgia law, what is the minimum notice a landlord must give a month-to-month residential tenant before terminating the tenancy?
choices: 30 days
7 days
15 days
60 days
explanation: Georgia's landlord-tenant law requires a minimum of 30 days written notice to terminate a month-to-month residential tenancy. For tenancies at-will in residential settings, 60 days notice is required under O.C.G.A. § 44-7-7.

question: In Georgia, which deed warranty provides the GREATEST protection to the grantee?
choices: Limited warranty deed (special warranty deed)
Quitclaim deed
Bargain and sale deed
General warranty deed
explanation: A general warranty deed (also called a 'warranty deed' in Georgia) provides the broadest protection: the grantor warrants title against all claims, including those arising before the grantor's ownership. A limited (special) warranty deed covers only claims arising during the grantor's ownership. A quitclaim deed provides no warranty.

question: A Georgia real estate licensee lists a property that has an on-site septic system that has been malfunctioning. The listing agent should:
choices: Keep it confidential since it is the seller's personal information
Disclose it only if required by the buyer's lender
Disclose it only after a buyer is under contract
Disclose the known defect to all prospective buyers as a material adverse fact
explanation: A malfunctioning septic system is an adverse material fact affecting the property's value and habitability. Under BRRETA, the listing agent must disclose all known adverse material facts to prospective buyers, regardless of the seller's preference for confidentiality.

question: Which Georgia statute requires disclosure of the existence of a homeowners association and its related fees to buyers?
choices: Georgia Property Owners' Association Act
Georgia Residential Mortgage Act
Georgia Condominium Act
Georgia Fair Housing Act
explanation: The Georgia Property Owners' Association Act (O.C.G.A. § 44-3-220 et seq.) governs planned communities with homeowners associations and requires disclosure of HOA existence, fees, and assessments to purchasers before closing.

question: Under Georgia's Condominium Act, a condominium developer must give a new buyer:
choices: A government-approved prospectus containing HOA financials
A 10-business-day rescission period after signing the purchase agreement
A site plan certified by a licensed engineer
A written property disclosure statement approved by GREC
explanation: Georgia's Condominium Act (O.C.G.A. § 44-3-111) gives buyers of new condominium units a 10-business-day rescission right after signing the purchase agreement or receiving the public offering statement (whichever is later), during which the buyer may cancel without penalty.

question: A Georgia listing agent's fiduciary duty of loyalty primarily obligates the agent to:
choices: Maximize the number of showings regardless of the seller's schedule
Disclose the seller's asking price to all buyers to stimulate competitive offers
Promote the seller's best interests above all others, excluding personal gain
Hold all offers for 24 hours before presenting to the seller
explanation: Under Georgia's client-representation framework, loyalty means placing the client's interests above those of all others — including the agent's own financial interests. The agent must not profit secretly, must not represent conflicting interests without disclosure, and must advocate for the client's position.

question: Georgia's Commercial Real Estate Broker Lien Act allows a commercial broker to:
choices: Report non-paying sellers to GREC for disciplinary action
File suit in federal court for unpaid commercial commissions
Place a lien on commercial property for an unpaid commission
Withhold a listing agreement until the commission is paid in full
explanation: The Georgia Commercial Real Estate Broker Lien Act (O.C.G.A. § 44-14-600 et seq.) gives commercial real estate brokers the right to place a lien against commercial property to secure payment of an earned but unpaid commission, similar to a mechanic's lien.

question: In Georgia, a listing agreement that automatically renews (evergreen clause) without the seller's affirmative action is:
choices: Permissible if the renewal period is 30 days or less
Prohibited — Georgia requires a definite expiration date in every exclusive listing
Required by MLS rules for all listed properties
Permissible for commercial properties but not residential
explanation: Georgia license law requires all exclusive listing agreements to contain a definite expiration date. Automatic renewal clauses ('evergreen' clauses) that extend the listing without the seller's affirmative act violate this requirement and are unenforceable.

question: A Georgia salesperson receives an earnest money check from a buyer on a Saturday. The following Monday is a federal holiday. The salesperson must place the funds in the broker's escrow account by:
choices: Friday — seven calendar days after receipt
Tuesday — the first banking day after the holiday
The end of business the same Saturday
Wednesday — three banking days after the Saturday of receipt
explanation: Georgia requires earnest money to be deposited within three banking days of receipt. Since Saturday is not a banking day and Monday is a holiday, the three-banking-day clock runs Tuesday, Wednesday — making Wednesday the deadline. The deposit must be made by close of business Wednesday.

question: Under the Georgia Uniform Fraudulent Transfers Act, a seller who transfers property to a family member for below-market consideration to avoid a creditor's claim may have the transfer:
choices: Set aside (voided) by the court as a fraudulent transfer
Approved by the court as a valid family gift
Protected from creditors for five years after the transfer date
Recorded without consequence if the deed is properly notarized
explanation: Georgia's Uniform Fraudulent Transfers Act allows courts to set aside (void) transfers made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors, including below-market transfers to family members made with fraudulent intent. Real estate licensees should be alert to such transactions.

question: Georgia's Multiple Listing Service (MLS) rules typically require that a new exclusive right-to-sell listing be submitted to the MLS within:
choices: 3 business days of the listing agreement being signed
30 days, but only if the seller has not waived MLS submission
10 days of the listing agreement being signed
24 hours of the listing agreement being signed
explanation: Most Georgia MLS organizations require submission of an exclusive right-to-sell listing within three business days of the listing agreement date, unless the seller has signed a written waiver delaying or waiving MLS entry. Delays beyond the MLS deadline may result in fines.

question: A Georgia licensee who has a personal financial interest in a property they are helping a client buy must:
choices: Obtain GREC's prior written approval to proceed
Withdraw from the transaction immediately
Require the client to hire an independent attorney
Disclose the personal interest in writing to the client before proceeding
explanation: BRRETA and Georgia license law require a licensee to disclose any personal interest in a transaction in writing before the client proceeds. The client may then decide whether to continue using the licensee or seek other representation. Disclosure, not automatic withdrawal, is the requirement.

question: Under Georgia law, a licensee who is also the seller's attorney in a transaction:
choices: Must choose one role — either licensee or attorney — before the transaction begins
Is automatically exempt from BRRETA requirements
May combine both roles freely without any disclosure
Must disclose both roles and obtain written consent from all parties
explanation: When a licensee serves dual roles (e.g., as both the real estate agent and the closing attorney), disclosure of the dual role and written informed consent of all parties is required. The Georgia Rules of Professional Conduct for attorneys also govern conflicts of interest.

question: In a Georgia purchase transaction, the security deed is recorded in the county where:
choices: The property is situated
The buyer's primary residence is located
The lender's principal office is located
The closing attorney maintains their law office
explanation: Real property instruments, including security deeds, are recorded in the Superior Court Clerk's office of the county where the property is located. Georgia follows the principle that land records are maintained in the county of the property's situs.

question: Georgia's intangible recording tax on a $300,000 mortgage is:
explanation: Georgia's intangible recording tax is $1.50 per $500 of the loan amount. $300,000 ÷ $500 = 600 units × $1.50 = $900. This tax is paid at recording and is typically a buyer/borrower cost.

question: A Georgia seller's real estate transfer tax on a $250,000 sale is:
explanation: Georgia's transfer tax is $1.00 per $500 (or fraction). $250,000 ÷ $500 = 500 units × $1.00 = $500. The seller is statutorily responsible for this tax, though it may be negotiated.

question: Georgia's right of redemption after a non-judicial foreclosure sale is:
choices: 12 months from the date of sale, as in most judicial foreclosure states
30 days, but only if the deficiency exceeds 20% of the loan balance
6 months if the borrower was current on taxes at the time of foreclosure
Extremely limited — Georgia does not provide a post-sale statutory redemption period in most cases
explanation: Unlike many states, Georgia does not provide a statutory post-sale redemption period for most residential non-judicial foreclosures. Once the property is sold at foreclosure, the borrower generally cannot redeem it. This differs significantly from judicial-foreclosure states with lengthy redemption windows.

question: After a non-judicial foreclosure sale in Georgia, a lender who receives less than the outstanding loan balance may pursue the borrower for a:
choices: Deficiency judgment, if sought within 30 days of the foreclosure sale
Recoupment only through the Georgia Real Estate Recovery Fund
Criminal fraud claim if the borrower stopped making payments intentionally
Deficiency judgment through a separate court action, subject to fair-value limitations
explanation: Georgia allows deficiency judgments after non-judicial foreclosure, but the lender must file a separate court action within 30 days of the sale. The court must determine the fair market value of the property; the deficiency is limited to the difference between the debt and the fair market value, not the sale price.

question: In Georgia, which lien has priority over all others when there is no contrary agreement?
choices: A materialman's (mechanic's) lien if work began before the mortgage was recorded
Property tax liens — they are superior to all other liens
A state income tax lien
A first mortgage lien recorded before other liens
explanation: In Georgia, property tax liens are superior (senior) to all other liens, regardless of the date of recording. Even a properly recorded first mortgage is subordinate to an ad valorem property tax lien. Failure to pay property taxes can result in a tax sale that extinguishes junior interests.

question: Georgia's homestead exemption primarily reduces a homeowner's:
choices: Capital gains on the sale of the primary residence
Mortgage interest rate
Title insurance premium at closing
Ad valorem property tax assessed value
explanation: Georgia's homestead exemption reduces the assessed value of a homeowner's primary residence for ad valorem (property tax) purposes. The basic exemption is $2,000 off assessed value; additional exemptions exist for seniors, veterans, and disabled individuals.

question: Georgia property is assessed for ad valorem tax purposes at what percentage of its fair market value?
explanation: Georgia law requires that real property be assessed at 40% of its fair market value for ad valorem tax purposes. The millage rate is then applied to this assessed value (40% of FMV) to determine the annual tax bill.

question: A Georgia property has a fair market value of $350,000. Using the standard 40% assessment ratio and a millage rate of 30 mills, what is the estimated annual property tax?
Under Georgia law, who is primarily responsible for paying the closing attorney's fee?
choices: The buyer, because the attorney typically represents the buyer's lender and/or the buyer
It is always split equally between buyer and seller
The seller, because they hired the attorney to prepare the deed
The real estate broker, as part of the commission
explanation: In most Georgia residential closings, the buyer (or the buyer's lender) selects and pays the closing attorney. The attorney represents the lender's interests in ensuring title and the loan documents are in order; the buyer is also a client. The seller may have separate counsel if desired.

question: In a Georgia RESPA-governed residential closing, the lender must provide the Closing Disclosure to the buyer at least:
choices: 3 business days before consummation
5 business days before the scheduled closing date
7 calendar days before closing
24 hours before closing
explanation: Under RESPA and the TRID (Know Before You Owe) rules implemented by the CFPB, the lender must provide the Closing Disclosure to the buyer at least three business days before consummation of the mortgage loan. This is a federal rule applied at all Georgia closings involving a regulated mortgage.

question: Under Georgia law, a deed must be signed by the grantor and acknowledged before a notary public. The deed becomes effective to transfer title when:
choices: It is acknowledged before a notary
It is delivered to and accepted by the grantee
It is signed by the grantor
It is recorded in the county deed records
explanation: In Georgia, as with general property law, a deed transfers title upon delivery to and acceptance by the grantee. Recording is necessary to protect the grantee's title against subsequent purchasers and creditors (constructive notice), but the actual transfer occurs at delivery and acceptance.

question: Georgia's 'race-notice' recording statute means that in a dispute between two purchasers of the same property, who prevails?
choices: The first buyer to sign a purchase contract
The buyer who records first, regardless of notice of prior claims
The buyer who paid a higher price for the property
The buyer who records first AND had no notice of the prior unrecorded transfer
explanation: Georgia follows a race-notice recording statute: a subsequent purchaser prevails over an earlier unrecorded transfer only if they recorded first AND took without notice (actual or constructive) of the prior transfer. A subsequent buyer with knowledge of an unrecorded prior deed cannot cut off the earlier buyer's interest.

question: Which of the following liens would a title search in Georgia MOST likely fail to uncover?
choices: A lis pendens notice recorded last month
An unrecorded mechanic's lien from a contractor paid in cash
A recorded first mortgage from five years ago
A recorded tax lien from the county
explanation: An unrecorded mechanic's (materialman's) lien would not appear in a standard title search of the public records because it has not been filed. Georgia allows materialmen a period of time to file a lien after completing work; during that window, an unrecorded lien is a risk a title search cannot detect — highlighting the value of title insurance.

question: Under Georgia license law, a real estate broker who wants to change the name of the firm must:
choices: Simply begin using the new name and notify clients informally
Apply to GREC for a new license reflecting the new firm name
Wait until the next scheduled renewal to change the name
Only notify the local MLS — GREC does not track firm names
explanation: Any change to the firm name requires an application to GREC for a new or amended license. The firm must use the name exactly as it appears on the license in all advertising and business activities.

question: Georgia license law requires every real estate firm to display its license:
choices: In a prominent location at each place of business
On all signage placed on listed properties
Only when GREC requests it during an inspection
Only on the firm's website
explanation: Georgia rules require that the firm's license (or branch certificate) be prominently displayed at each office location. This requirement ensures that clients and the public can verify the firm's credentials.

question: A Georgia salesperson who leaves a brokerage on bad terms and takes the client list is:
choices: Only liable to the broker in a civil suit, not subject to license discipline
Subject to GREC discipline for misappropriating the broker's business records
Violating their fiduciary duty to the clients who must be notified
Within their rights, since the salesperson cultivated the client relationships
explanation: Client lists and transaction records belong to the qualifying broker, not the individual salesperson. Taking the broker's business records without authorization is a violation of license law and may subject the salesperson to GREC discipline in addition to civil liability.

question: Under Georgia law, practicing real estate without a license is classified as a:
choices: Class C misdemeanor
Civil infraction with a maximum fine of $5,000
Felony in all circumstances
Class A misdemeanor
explanation: Georgia classifies unlicensed real estate practice as a misdemeanor (high and aggravated in some circumstances). Repeat violations or conduct involving fraud can escalate to felony charges. The criminal penalties underscore the seriousness of the licensing requirement.

question: GREC has the authority to investigate a licensee:
choices: Only if a criminal charge has been filed against the licensee
On its own initiative (sua sponte) or upon complaint by any person
Only upon receipt of a written, sworn complaint from a party to a transaction
Only if the alleged violation resulted in a financial loss exceeding $10,000
explanation: GREC may investigate a licensee on its own initiative or upon receipt of a complaint from any person. There is no requirement that the complainant be a party to a transaction or that a specific financial threshold be met.

question: A Georgia licensee who has been convicted of a crime must notify GREC:
choices: Within 10 days of the conviction or guilty plea
Within 30 days if the crime involved moral turpitude only
Only at the next license renewal
There is no self-reporting requirement; GREC discovers convictions through background checks
explanation: Georgia requires licensees to notify GREC within 10 days of any conviction, guilty plea, or nolo contendere plea to a felony or any crime involving moral turpitude. Failure to self-report is itself a separate disciplinary violation.

question: Under Georgia license law, a qualifying broker may be disciplined for the acts of an affiliated salesperson:
choices: Only if the broker had actual knowledge and failed to intervene
Only if the broker personally directed the salesperson's unlawful conduct
When the broker knew or should have known of the conduct and failed to take reasonable corrective action
Never — each licensee is individually responsible for their own conduct
explanation: Georgia holds qualifying brokers accountable for the supervised activities of their affiliated licensees when the broker knew or reasonably should have known of unlawful conduct and failed to take corrective action. This supervisory liability encourages brokers to implement robust compliance programs.

question: Which of the following is TRUE about an inactive Georgia real estate license?
choices: The holder must surrender the license to GREC and reapply when ready to practice
The holder may continue to perform real estate activities at a reduced scope
The license automatically converts to active status after 12 months of inactivity
The holder may not engage in any brokerage activities and must pay reduced renewal fees
explanation: An inactive Georgia license allows the holder to maintain their license in good standing without affiliating with a broker, but prohibits them from performing any real estate brokerage activities. Renewal fees for inactive licenses are typically lower; CE may still be required.

question: A Georgia real estate licensee who is also a licensed auctioneer may legally sell real estate at auction:
choices: Without any license if the auction is conducted at a county courthouse
Only under the real estate license, because real estate auctions require a real estate license
Under the auctioneer license alone, without engaging the real estate license
Under either license for personal property, but only the real estate license for real property
explanation: Auctioning real property for compensation in Georgia requires a real estate license. A licensee who also holds an auctioneer's license must activate the real estate license for real property auctions. The auctioneer license alone is insufficient for real estate.

question: The Georgia Real Estate Commission may reinstate a revoked license if the licensee:
choices: Appeals to the Superior Court within 30 days of revocation
Completes 100 hours of continuing education within 12 months of revocation
Pays a reinstatement fine equal to two years of license fees
Demonstrates rehabilitation and meets any conditions set by GREC
explanation: GREC has discretionary authority to reinstate a revoked license if the former licensee demonstrates rehabilitation and meets any conditions GREC establishes, such as repayment to the Recovery Fund, additional education, or a supervised probationary period.

question: Under Georgia rules, a real estate licensee must provide a written agency disclosure to a prospective buyer:
choices: At or before the time a brokerage engagement is signed
Before showing the first property
Within 24 hours of the first contact
Only when a purchase offer is being prepared
explanation: BRRETA requires the written brokerage engagement be executed at or before the time a client signs an offer or enters a listing agreement. Georgia does not require a pre-showing disclosure form, but licensees commonly explain the relationship at first contact as a best practice.

question: A Georgia broker who discovers that a former salesperson forged client signatures on earnest money receipts should:
choices: Issue an internal reprimand and require the salesperson to repeat the ethics course
Consult their E&O carrier and take no other action
Quietly terminate the salesperson and correct the records without reporting
Report the misconduct to GREC and cooperate with any investigation
explanation: Discovery of forgery or fraud obligates a qualifying broker to report the misconduct to GREC and cooperate fully with the investigation. Concealing misconduct to avoid embarrassment or liability could itself constitute a violation of the broker's supervisory duties.

question: Under Georgia's net listing rules, net listings are:
choices: Prohibited by GREC rules as creating an inherent conflict of interest
Permitted and commonly used for commercial property
Permitted only if the seller is represented by an attorney
Permitted for vacant land transactions only
explanation: Net listings — where the broker's compensation is the amount by which the sale price exceeds a minimum set by the seller — are prohibited by GREC rules. They create an inherent conflict between the broker's duty to the client and the broker's financial self-interest.

question: Which of the following is an example of 'misrepresentation' under Georgia license law?
choices: Telling a buyer that a property is in 'excellent condition' when the agent knows the roof leaks
Listing a property at a price higher than the agent's recommended value
Failing to disclose the seller's asking price to a prospective buyer
Declining to show a property that is outside the buyer's stated budget
explanation: Misrepresentation is making a false or misleading statement of a material fact. Telling a buyer the property is in excellent condition while knowing of a leaking roof is fraudulent misrepresentation — a grounds for disciplinary action and civil liability.

question: A Georgia real estate firm must maintain its escrow (trust) accounts at:
choices: The firm's primary operating bank, as a subaccount
Any nationally chartered bank, regardless of location
A credit union approved by the Georgia Department of Banking
A federally insured financial institution located in Georgia
explanation: Georgia rules require that escrow accounts be held in a federally insured financial institution doing business in Georgia. The account must be maintained in the state to ensure regulatory access and protect client funds.

question: Which of the following best describes Georgia's 'broker price opinion' (BPO) rules?
choices: Licensees may prepare BPOs as long as they are not used as appraisals for federally related mortgage transactions
BPOs require a separate GREC-issued BPO certification
BPOs are prohibited in Georgia under the Appraiser Independence Requirements
Only appraisers may prepare BPOs; licensees may not
explanation: Georgia licensees may prepare broker price opinions (BPOs) for purposes such as listing pricing, investor analysis, or relocation. BPOs may not substitute for licensed appraisals in federally related mortgage transactions. No separate GREC BPO certification is required.

question: Under BRRETA, if a Georgia broker wants to represent both the buyer and the seller in the same transaction as a disclosed dual agent, the broker must obtain:
choices: Written informed consent from both the buyer and the seller
Oral consent from both parties before showing any property
A dual-agency endorsement on the broker's license
GREC's prior approval of the dual-agency arrangement
explanation: Georgia BRRETA requires written informed consent from both the buyer client and the seller client before a broker may act as a disclosed dual agent. Without written consent, the broker cannot lawfully represent both parties in the same transaction.

question: A Georgia listing agent receives an offer from an unrepresented buyer (customer). Under BRRETA, the agent's obligation to the unrepresented buyer includes:
choices: Confidentiality about the seller's situation and no duty to disclose defects
Honesty, presenting all offers to the seller, and disclosing adverse material facts
No obligations whatsoever, since the buyer chose not to hire an agent
Full fiduciary duties, identical to those owed to the seller client
explanation: Even for customers (non-clients), BRRETA imposes baseline obligations: honesty in all dealings, timely presentation of all written offers, and disclosure of adverse material facts known to the broker. These minimum duties apply regardless of representation.

question: In Georgia, an option to purchase real estate must be:
choices: Notarized and filed with the county recorder
Approved by the applicable city or county planning authority
In writing, signed by the optionor, and supported by consideration
Recorded within 10 days to be enforceable against third parties
explanation: A valid real estate option in Georgia must be in writing (Statute of Frauds), signed by the optionor (property owner), and supported by some consideration. No recording is required for enforceability between the parties, though recording provides constructive notice to third parties.

question: Georgia's Residential Property Condition Disclosure — when voluntarily used by a seller — most commonly covers:
choices: Title defects and encumbrances affecting the property
Only structural defects that affect the foundation
Environmental contamination discovered by licensed inspectors
The seller's actual knowledge of material defects and property conditions
explanation: The GREC-approved disclosure form asks sellers to disclose their actual knowledge of material defects and conditions in the property — roof condition, HVAC systems, plumbing, electrical, past flooding, pests, and so on. It represents the seller's knowledge, not a professional inspection result.

question: A Georgia broker's trust account earns interest. Under Georgia law, interest earned on client funds held in escrow:
choices: Belongs to the broker as additional compensation
Belongs to the client whose funds are on deposit, unless otherwise agreed
Must be donated to a HUD-approved housing counseling agency
Must be paid to the Georgia Real Estate Commission
explanation: Interest earned on funds held in escrow belongs to the client whose funds generated the interest, unless all parties have expressly agreed otherwise in writing. A broker who retains interest without authorization is engaging in a form of conversion.

question: Which Georgia entity is responsible for regulating mortgage loan originators?
choices: Georgia Department of Community Affairs
Georgia Real Estate Commission (GREC)
Georgia Department of Banking and Finance
Georgia Secretary of State's office
explanation: The Georgia Department of Banking and Finance regulates mortgage loan originators and mortgage lenders/brokers under the Georgia Residential Mortgage Act. GREC regulates real estate licensees; these are separate regulatory systems.

question: A Georgia property management company receives a rent check after the first of the month. Under standard Georgia lease terms, the landlord should:
choices: Immediately deposit it as earned income for that period
Hold it for 30 days to verify funds before depositing
Apply it to outstanding late fees first, then to rent
Follow the terms of the lease, which typically specify a grace period and late fee schedule
explanation: Georgia property managers must follow the lease terms, which define the due date, grace period, and late fee provisions. Many Georgia leases provide a grace period (often 5 days) before a late fee applies. The lease governs the treatment of late rent payments.

question: The Georgia Fair Housing Act mirrors the federal Fair Housing Act but also protects individuals based on:
choices: Marital status and source of income in all jurisdictions statewide
Additional classes designated by individual county ordinances only
The same classes as the federal Act — no additional state-level protected classes exist statewide
Sexual orientation, as added by the Georgia Legislature in 2010
explanation: Georgia's state Fair Housing Act substantially mirrors the federal law and does not add protected classes at the statewide level. Some Georgia municipalities have added local fair housing protections (e.g., sexual orientation), but there are no additional statewide protected classes beyond the federal seven.

question: Which of the following exemptions applies to Georgia's Fair Housing law for owner-occupied housing?
choices: An owner-occupied single-family dwelling sold or rented without a broker and without discriminatory advertising may qualify for an exemption
Owner-occupants may legally refuse to rent to families with children in all cases
All owner-occupied housing is exempt from fair housing requirements
Exemptions for owner-occupied housing apply only to properties with more than four units
explanation: Georgia (following federal law) provides a limited exemption for owner-occupied single-family homes sold or rented by the owner without using a real estate broker AND without discriminatory public advertising. Once a broker is involved, all fair housing protections apply fully.

question: In Georgia, real estate purchase contracts typically specify earnest money as 'liquidated damages.' This means:
choices: The earnest money is always sufficient to compensate the seller for any loss
The buyer automatically forfeits the earnest money for any breach, regardless of circumstances
If the buyer defaults, the seller's sole remedy is to retain the earnest money as agreed compensation for the breach
The seller must return the earnest money if the buyer provides 30 days notice of cancellation
explanation: When earnest money is specified as liquidated damages in a Georgia purchase contract, the seller's remedy for a buyer default is limited to retaining the earnest money — the seller cannot simultaneously sue for additional damages (unless the contract provides otherwise). This provides certainty for both parties.

question: Under Georgia law, specific performance is an available remedy when a seller wrongfully refuses to convey property after a valid contract. Specific performance requires:
choices: Arbitration of the dispute before filing suit
That the buyer demonstrate the property is unique and monetary damages are inadequate
A monetary deposit equal to 10% of the purchase price as security
GREC's written finding that the seller violated license law
explanation: Specific performance in Georgia requires the buyer to show that the real property is unique (as all land is considered unique) and that monetary damages would be inadequate. Courts may order a seller to convey the property per the contract terms. This remedy is available for real estate because land is deemed legally unique.

question: Under Georgia's eviction law, after a landlord serves a valid demand for possession for nonpayment of rent and the tenant fails to pay or vacate, the landlord must:
choices: Contact GREC for mediation before proceeding with eviction
File a dispossessory (eviction) action in the appropriate court
Wait 30 days before taking any further legal action
Change the locks immediately after the demand period expires
explanation: After a proper demand for possession goes unanswered, Georgia requires the landlord to file a dispossessory action (eviction lawsuit) in the appropriate court — typically Magistrate Court. Self-help evictions (changing locks, removing belongings) are illegal in Georgia.

question: A Georgia broker discovers that the property she listed contains an underground storage tank (UST). She should:
choices: Keep the information confidential to protect the seller's sale price
Remove the tank before the listing goes live
Disclose it only after an offer is received and the buyer is committed
Disclose the existence of the UST as a potential adverse material fact
explanation: An underground storage tank is a potential environmental hazard and adverse material fact that may affect the property's value, insurability, and usability. BRRETA requires disclosure of known adverse material facts; the broker must disclose the known UST to prospective buyers.

question: Under BRRETA, the duty to 'timely present' offers and counteroffers means a Georgia broker must:
choices: Present all offers within 24 hours of receipt, regardless of the client's instructions
Present only offers that meet or exceed the listed price
Present offers promptly without delay, unless the client has given specific written instructions otherwise
Hold all offers and present them simultaneously at an open-offer meeting
explanation: BRRETA requires brokers to present all written offers and counteroffers promptly (timely) to the client. The client may give written instructions about how or when to present offers (e.g., 'hold all offers until Sunday'), and the broker must follow those lawful instructions.

question: A Georgia real estate broker may lawfully receive compensation in a transaction from:
choices: Only the seller, regardless of who the broker represents
Any party, as long as all parties are informed in writing
Only the party who signs the listing agreement
Only the party the broker represents
explanation: Georgia brokers may accept compensation from any party to the transaction — including the seller, buyer, or both — as long as all parties are informed of the compensation arrangement in writing. Undisclosed dual compensation is prohibited.

question: Georgia's Homestead Exemption filing deadline for the following tax year is typically:
choices: December 31
March 1
June 1
April 1
explanation: In most Georgia counties, the homestead exemption application must be filed by April 1 to apply to that year's tax bill. However, March 1 is the deadline in some counties. Buyers who close near year-end should file immediately to qualify for the upcoming tax year. The standard statewide deadline is April 1.

question: Georgia's documentary stamp tax on deeds and mortgages is more commonly called the:
choices: Conveyance tax
Deed registration fee
Real estate transfer tax
Intangible recording tax
explanation: Georgia's tax on the transfer of real property is commonly referred to as the 'real estate transfer tax' or, in some contexts, the 'documentary stamp tax.'
